title: A Full Circle to Voorhees College
summary: Sellers describes the full circle of his life in Denmark, South Carolina, from college mascot at three to president of Voorhees in 2008, growing up in a rigidly segregated town that was long majority Black yet held every official office in white hands.

title: An Education That Reached the World
summary: He recalls the Booker T. Washington model of the Voorhees-affiliated school, where teachers instilled education, faith, and service, where students openly discussed Emmett Till from the cover of Jet and projected his memory into a resolve to find remedies, and where African independence and figures like Nkrumah were common talk.

title: Family, Faith, and a Father in France
summary: Sellers traces his entrepreneur father, a World War II veteran of France who built rental property and a motel, his educator mother who ran a recreation center and mentored young women, and a churchgoing family that moved through AME, Baptist, and the Episcopal Church he has kept since.

title: The Sit-Ins Come to Denmark
summary: A high schooler working the student center, Sellers watched the February 1960 Greensboro sit-ins fill the room each night, and recounts the Voorhees students marching downtown in their Sunday best after the president quietly withdrew the bus, being arrested and retrieved by the college priest.

title: A Father's Objection, a School's Rejection
summary: Sellers describes organizing an NAACP youth chapter and his father forbidding him to attend its rally, his frustration, and his rejected application to a Virginia military academy that turned him away once he answered that he was Negro, deepening his search for a place to be his own person in the movement.

title: Jail-No-Bail at Rock Hill
summary: He recounts taking carloads of Denmark students to Rock Hill, where Charles Jones, Diane Nash, and Ruby Doris Smith had come from Atlanta and gone to jail in the jail-no-bail stand, his first link to SNCC, amid a growing wave of South Carolina activity from Charleston to Clemson.

part: Howard and the Nonviolent Action Group
title: A Bourgeois Campus, and Jeans
summary: Sellers arrived at Howard in 1962 expecting a hotbed of activity and instead found a professional, status-seeking campus where his Southern jeans and longer hair drew friction, and where he came to feel his role was to push the whole process open rather than become a token.

part: Howard and the Nonviolent Action Group
title: NAG, and Bringing Malcolm to Howard
summary: He describes finding his niche in the Nonviolent Action Group with Mike Thelwell and Charlie Cobb, picketing the Justice Department, FBI, and White House and taking up DC home rule, placing members in the campus newspaper and government, and pushing the administration to host first Bayard Rustin and then Malcolm X.

part: Howard and the Nonviolent Action Group
title: The March on Washington and the 16th Street Bombing
summary: Sellers recounts working the March on Washington logistics under Bayard Rustin, the controversy over John Lewis's speech and A. Philip Randolph's personal appeal to soften it, the euphoria of the day, and how the 16th Street Baptist Church bombing shattered it and pushed the Emmett Till generation toward full-time work.

title: Recruiting for Freedom Summer
summary: Assigned by Bob Moses to generate volunteers for Freedom Summer, Sellers and NAG recruited from the Maryland and DC campuses, leveraging the largest contingent of African American volunteers to get into the plantation black belt of Mississippi's second congressional district.

title: The Cambridge Movement and George Wallace
summary: He describes the violent Cambridge, Maryland movement under martial law, Gloria Richardson's radicalism, and the night George Wallace came to town when the National Guard used a nauseating tear gas, a SNCC photographer was bayoneted, and Sellers was arrested with Stokely Carmichael and held in the armory.

title: The Three Who Did Not Return
summary: At the Ohio orientation Sellers learned a church had been burned in Philadelphia and saw Chaney, Schwerner, and Goodman off to investigate, and recounts Bob Moses gathering everyone to say the three were missing and presumed dead, and the volunteers' choice, after a song, of whether to still go.
related_events: Murders of Chaney, Goodman, and Schwerner

title: Searching the Barns by Night
summary: He describes the collective who drove into Mississippi from four directions to search for the bodies, hiding by day in barn lofts and going out at night with maps and no lights, watching for the fire towers, until after two nights they pulled the plug to spare the farmers who sheltered them.
related_events: Murders of Chaney, Goodman, and Schwerner

title: The Holly Springs Project
summary: Sellers describes the large Holly Springs project spanning several counties, with master teachers, a nurse, a freedom school, and a community center, the freedom days that took people to register at the cost of their names in the paper, and the young people like Roy DeBerry it sent off to college.

title: Working His Way Out of a Job
summary: He explains taking over as project director from Ivanhoe Donaldson and the philosophy of organizing communities so local people like Rita could take over, working their way out of a job, and the records the group archived at Rust College that turned Holly Springs into a progressive city.

title: The Burned Station Wagon on Display
summary: Sellers recounts documenting the summer in WATS logs and the disloyal, openly discriminatory regular Democratic Party, and hauling the bell from the burned Philadelphia church and the murdered workers' burned-out station wagon to Atlantic City to show the level of violence used to defend segregation.

title: The Compromise and Fannie Lou Hamer
summary: He describes the Johnson administration handing the convention to Humphrey and Mondale, the two-seats-at-large compromise, the parade of coalition figures sent to sell it as a victory, and how Johnson called a press conference to bump Fannie Lou Hamer's I-question-America testimony off the air.

title: Devastated by Power Politics
summary: Sellers recalls being proud of the delegation's refusal yet devastated that morality was not the basis of decisions inside the Democratic Party, a turn that pushed SNCC to set the party aside and look outward toward self-determination and movements in Africa, Asia, and Cuba.

title: John Lewis and Bloody Sunday
summary: He explains SNCC's strategic difference with SCLC, organizing versus mobilizing, and how the group reluctantly let chairman John Lewis march in Selma on his own conscience, then rushed in after he suffered a concussion on Bloody Sunday, an operational principle of never leaving one of their own unsupported.

title: On Different Pages in Selma
summary: Sellers describes taking up the march logistics with Hosea Williams, the anger when King crossed the bridge and turned back without telling SNCC the plan, Forman opening another front in Montgomery, and the underlying tension that SNCC, two years into building Selma, knew a march would dissipate without organization.

title: Building the Lowndes County Freedom Organization
summary: He recounts the turn to independent political organizing in Lowndes County, Jack Minnis's discovery of a Civil War-era Alabama law allowing a separate county party, and how Sellers marshaled the declining organization's resources and NAG veterans to build the Lowndes County Freedom Organization around empowerment.

title: How the Black Panther Got Its Name
summary: Sellers explains how the media labeled the Lowndes County Freedom Organization the Black Panther Party, how the young poll watchers who came to help carried the symbol back to California, Chicago, and New York where Bobby Seale's Oakland party adopted the model, and how the county's voters, defeated the first time, later won office.

title: Black Power and the Distorted Position Paper
summary: He describes SNCC's 1967 transformation, the killing of Sammy Younge that prompted its anti-war statement, success scattering people, the anti-war movement drawing off white students, and how the Atlanta Project's never-adopted position paper distorted Black Power into a narrow color analysis SNCC had actually fought.

title: COINTELPRO and Three Months in Prison
summary: Sellers underscores how badly people underestimate COINTELPRO's effect, and recounts being held in prison for three months in the summer of 1967 over his draft induction, denied bail as a risk until Justice Black ordered his release, time he says he gets credit for nowhere.

title: The Truth About King and Black Power
summary: Sellers corrects the myth of antagonism between SNCC and Dr. King, recounting the long basement evening when King asked Stokely Carmichael and him to explain Black Power, their handshake never to condemn one another, King inviting them to his Vietnam announcement, and Stokely's help at Resurrection City.

title: The University of the Movement
summary: With the Orangeburg massacre and prison left for a later session, Sellers reflects on the movement as a university where he learned from King, Malcolm X, Ella Baker, Fannie Lou Hamer, and Ruby Doris Robinson, his master's at Harvard and doctorate at UNC, and points to his autobiography The River of No Return.
